title: St. Helena the Empress — St. Christopher (Monthly) & Sts. Shmona and Guria
primary_saint: St. Helena the Empress and Equal-to-the-Apostles, the Finder of the Holy Cross, St. Christopher the Great Martyr, and Sts. Shmona and Guria the Confessors of Edessa
narrative:
On the ninth of Ginbot, the holy Church celebrates the feast of the holy and right-believing Empress Helena, mother of the Emperor Constantine and Equal-to-the-Apostles, the woman who in her old age — she was nearly eighty when she made the journey — descended upon Jerusalem with imperial resources and an apostolic determination, excavated beneath the Temple of Venus that Hadrian had built over Golgotha, and discovered the wood of the True Cross together with the tomb of the Resurrection. Helena is one of the great figures of the fourth century not merely for what she found but for what her finding meant: she is the woman who restored to the Christian people the material evidence of their salvation, who insisted that the faith was not only spiritual but physical, not only interior but historical, not only a matter of proclamation but of place and wood and stone. Her discovery of the Cross is the Church's annual reminder that the Incarnation has sanctified matter itself, that the wood that bore the body of the Lord carries a holiness that outlasts the centuries.

The theological significance of Helena in the Ethiopian tradition is amplified by her position as the mother of the first Christian emperor. Her pilgrimage to Jerusalem was not merely personal piety but imperial statement: the most powerful woman in the world prostrating herself before the empty tomb, financing the construction of the great basilicas, distributing her personal wealth to the poor of Palestine, releasing prisoners from the imperial jails as an act of Paschal mercy. The tradition sees in her the fulfilment of the prophecy that kings would bring their glory into the New Jerusalem: an empress becoming a pilgrim, power becoming service, wealth becoming oblation. Helena is the model for all subsequent royal and imperial patronage of the Church, and every church built by a king or queen in Ethiopia stands in the tradition she inaugurated.

Saint Christopher receives his monthly commemoration on this day — his primary feast having been celebrated on Ginbot 6 in the translation of his relics — and the tradition uses the monthly occasion to develop what the primary feast left implicit: the Christological meaning of his name and legend. Christopher, the Christ-bearer, is understood in the Ethiopian tradition through the lens of his most famous legend: the giant who stood in a river and carried travellers across, who one night carried a child so heavy that he nearly buckled under the weight, and who discovered that the child was the Christ carrying the weight of the world. This legend is not mythology but theology: it is the tradition's pictorial statement that the burden of Christ is the burden of all human sorrow, that to carry Christ is to carry everything, and that the one who submits to that weight will be strengthened by it rather than crushed.

Saints Shmona and Guria are the great discovery of this day: two confessors of Edessa in Syria who were imprisoned during the persecution of Diocletian and whose long imprisonment was the occasion for one of the most remarkable pastoral correspondences in early Christian literature. Habib, a deacon of Edessa who was later martyred himself, wrote to the two imprisoned confessors from outside the prison, and they wrote back; the three documents — two letters from the prisoners and one from the deacon — constitute a window into what sustained faith looked like from inside the experience of persecution, written not by bishops composing formal treatises but by ordinary believers trying to hold each other up from opposite sides of a prison wall. Shmona and Guria were eventually executed by strangulation. Their feast alongside Helena and Christopher is the tradition's statement about the two faces of Christian witness: the empress who builds basilicas and the prisoners who write letters are equally essential to the Church's life, and the faith that Helena found written in wood and stone was the same faith that Shmona and Guria wrote to each other through the bars of their cell. Amen.
